posts.name: Glenn L e v i n e
posts.cooked:
A set of 2012s in this house, 4 bottles of each now imbibed and loved since holiday time last year. So it’s 13 months, don’t make a federal case…

• 2012 Ceritas Porter-Bass Chardonnay. Like the 07 this wine just jumped from the case screaming drink me! Beginning to lose the exuberance now and here’s hoping the other eight bottles shine in a new way the next few years.

• 2012 Hanzell Estate Chardonnay is just the loveliest drop from this venerable Sonoma property since the 01 PN maybe? It is so clean and pure and hasn’t moved off the mark much at all over this year. A 5th bottle is a possibility tonight.

• 2012 Bedrock North Coast Syrah is just a sensation. How do these men make a wine this great and resist the urge to charge more for every bottle? Four for four here in the land where Syrah season doesn’t end. A real achievement.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
Markus S:

I don’t know how you guys do it, you must really believe in “the daily drinker”. Personally, I cannot fathom going through more than 2 bottles of anything in the same year, no matter how much I enjoy it, rather, I would like to try them over the following year and the year after that to see how they change(d) or simply try something new, as I tend to be bored drinking the same wine day-in and day-out. Of course, I very rarely buy case quantities of anything.

You just buy enough to drink it regularly and also follow it over time.

At this point in my wine drinking life, I know what I like. Of course, my cellar is large enough to have plenty of variety across producers, vineyards and vintages; but within certain categories, I don’t need to actively seek out dozens of new things. For instance, every time I drink a good rosé champagne NV that isn’t Egly-Ouriet, I think “this is good, but I’d kinda rather be drinking Egly.” Why buy variety when I can always have the one I like best?

I end up tasting many new things anyway, as they come along from friends or on a wine list or while traveling, and that is fun. Sometimes I even encounter a wine or a producer I want to add to my cellar, which is especially fun. But for the most part, I am thrilled to get to be good friends with particular wines. We visit often, and our relationship will last for years.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Markus S:

I don’t know how you guys do it, you must really believe in “the daily drinker”. Personally, I cannot fathom going through more than 2 bottles of anything in the same year, no matter how much I enjoy it, rather, I would like to try them over the following year and the year after that to see how they change(d) or simply try something new, as I tend to be bored drinking the same wine day-in and day-out. Of course, I very rarely buy case quantities of anything.

Fair question, but as in most things in life, it’s a personal preference. After almost 25 years of doing this thing - and daily consumption of wine - I know what I like and have a narrow palate preference. Even if I go through a case of something in a year, that’s still only one bottle a month. This week I popped consequently the 2007 and 2010 Raffault Les Picasses, even sampling side by side. Loved both. For many years I tried a lot of experimentation, but it has led me here. My only gripe is not having enough truly mature wines!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Probably either the Ulysse Collin Pierrieres Blanc de Blancs or the 2006 Clos Roillette Tardive… Also worked through five magnums of the 1970 Pape Clement very contentedly, as the wine is at peak.

If a wine is complex, expressive and fulfilling an appreciable amount of its potential, I have no problem encountering it quite frequently: repeated exposure heightens one’s awareness of wine’s merits—and equally defects. I remember drinking a case of the 2004 Domaine de Chevalier rouge over the course of a year: I enjoyed the first few bottles, but drank them relatively uncritically; by the end of the case, the wine’s new oak had become obtrusively obvious—an old trick if you will. That was a teachable moment, and the experience is extensible to wines encountered less regularly.

2011 Dominique Piron Morgon Cote de Puy (8 bottles). Followed closely by
2011 Antonio Vallana e Figlio Spanna (7btls).

Sidenote: I picked up the Piron from Premier Cru. I also found Julien Sunier’s and Anne Sophie Dubois’ Beaujolais there for the first time. I will miss their excellent, good value, Bojo picks.

posts.cooked: As far as I can tell, I didn’t have any complete repeats (as in, same wine and vintage). I had a few where I had more than one vintage of the same wine. But my records for the first half of the year are spotty at best, and my inventory wasn’t very accurate until I did a big cleanup. I tend to not buy more than 3 bottles of anything unless it’s my absolute favorite wine, so I don’t repeat very often.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:
Markus S:

I don’t know how you guys do it, you must really believe in “the daily drinker”. Personally, I cannot fathom going through more than 2 bottles of anything in the same year, no matter how much I enjoy it, rather, I would like to try them over the following year and the year after that to see how they change(d) or simply try something new, as I tend to be bored drinking the same wine day-in and day-out. Of course, I very rarely buy case quantities of anything.

Fair question, but as in most things in life, it’s a personal preference. After almost 25 years of doing this thing - and daily consumption of wine - I know what I like and have a narrow palate preference. Even if I go through a case of something in a year, that’s still only one bottle a month. This week I popped consequently the 2007 and 2010 Raffault Les Picasses, even sampling side by side. Loved both. For many years I tried a lot of experimentation, but it has led me here. My only gripe is not having enough truly mature wines!

Well, it’s not that I don’t know what I like, since I’ve been at this about 30 years now, but it’s most likely because I get bored. Even the wines I love, I treasure them much more if I have them infrequently. And because the wine universe is constantly expanding (like the universe!), I am always finding “new paradigms” to quote that lovely phrase to challenge what I know and put a new spin on things. Probably, I could be content having the same-ol’-same-ol’, but with more new producers, new regions, newly found “old” grapes, new vintages, I simply find the wine world too interesting to drink case quantities on anything. Okay, maybe I would on Gonon (or Tempier or Musar …), or if the price was right, but not very often. I like and enjoy variety, even though there are the usual yearly purchases. Hope that explains my logic.
